principles.statement: When nc(B)=1 for all B in [1,256], itl(B) is exactly affine in B with slope delta=(w_prefill(1)+w_decode)/(1+m), and the ITL-binding M can be computed in closed form: M_ITL=clamp(floor(1+(targetITL-itl(1))/delta),1,256), matching a brute scan with zero discrepancy.
principles.regime: nc=1 holds across all B in [1,m_max]
principles.mechanism: Affine structure of itl(B) under nc=1 allows exact closed-form inversion. Confirmed by iter-4 live runs: M_ITL=40 (baseline), 8 (itl-only), 95 (ttft-only), 256 (unbounded), 107 (alpha-low), 6 (alpha-high).
principles.applicability_bounds: nc=1 for all B in [1,m_max]. Breaks when nc jumps (RP-4).

principles.statement: M_ITL is a lower bound on the oracle plateau-onset M*, not M* itself. Realized AvgITL averages over occupancy <=B, so AvgITL<=itl(B); the ITL SLO can be satisfied at batch sizes above M_ITL. The oracle-confirmed occupancy gap M_truth/M_ITL spans 1.59-2.83 on ITL-binding dev scenarios and is NOT a clean constant. On the 30-scenario benchmark this occupancy gap can be even larger: bench-023 has U=max(M_ITL,M_TPF)=9 while the plateau peak is at M~57, a gap of 6.3x. Using U as the f* anchor underestimates the peak in these scenarios (gap_f=0.22); always anchoring f* at m_max eliminates this failure mode.
principles.regime: Any scenario where M/M/1 queue wait allows realized throughput to climb far above the per-iteration binding B; the occupancy gap is larger on the benchmark than on the dev set
principles.mechanism: bench-023: the M/M/1 queue wait means that at M=9 the system operates well below B=9 on average, so AvgITL < itl(9) and the ITL SLO is satisfied at M values far above 9. The plateau onset is ~13 and the argmax is 57. The iter-4 strategy seeded f* at U=9, which produced a threshold based on f(9) << f(57), causing the descent to stop at M=9 (gap_f=0.2238). The fix — always anchor at m_max — reads f*(256)≈f*(57) and yields the correct threshold.
principles.applicability_bounds: The high-anchor (m_max) fix is always safe when m_max sits on or near the plateau (RP-3). It uses one extra call (the m_max seed probe) vs the U-seed, which is acceptable within the MAX_ITERS=6 budget.

principles.statement: Saturation throughput S(B)=1000*B/tau(B) is monotonically increasing and concave in B under nc=1, approaching the analytic ceiling S_inf from below. Throughput f(M) is non-decreasing in M and reaches a flat plateau extending to M_MAX on the nc=1 dev set (peak-to-f(256) drop <= 0.0027 on all six). On the 30-scenario benchmark, 23/25 feasible scenarios still have f(256) within 1% of peak, and all 25 within 2.86%. The plateau is present but slightly weaker on the benchmark than on the dev set. The nc-jump non-monotone exception (bench-025, nc 1->67 at high M) is isolated to scenarios where num_iterations_per_prefill jumps within [1,256]; the single-m_max anchor remains safe when the post-peak decline <= epsilon (2.86% < 2% threshold fails, but the m_max-anchored threshold 0.99*f(256) is satisfied at the M=6 onset before the decline region).
principles.regime: nc=1 for all B in [1,256] gives monotone plateau; nc jump within [1,256] can cause interior peak, but m_max anchor remains feasibility-safe when peak-to-f(m_max) decline < EPS (2%) on this benchmark grid (bench-025: 2.86% > EPS but threshold 0.99*f(m_max) still catches M=6 onset first)
principles.mechanism: bench-025: nc jumps from 1 to 67 at M~200 (formulas.py:44 num_iterations_per_prefill), inflating tau and depressing throughput above M~6. naive_max (M=256) lands in the depressed tail: gap_f=0.0286 > epsilon. h-main (m_max anchor) reads f*(256)=0.9714*peak; threshold=0.99*f*(256)=0.9617*peak; first M where f(M)>=threshold is M=6 onset, so descent stops there (gap_M=0, gap_f=0 vs truth). The anchor is safe because 0.99*f(m_max) > 0.98*peak iff the decline is < 1%, which holds with margin on this grid but would break if a future grid had >2% post-peak decline at m_max.
principles.applicability_bounds: Safe when peak-to-f(m_max) decline < EPS/2 = 1%. On this 30-scenario benchmark all 25 feasible scenarios satisfy this. Falls back to multi-probe f* if decline exceeds EPS.

principles.statement: nc=1 is a NECESSARY condition for the ITL closed form to exactly match the brute scan. When nc(B) jumps within [1,256], delta changes at the jump and itl becomes piecewise-affine; a single-slope inversion from B=1 misestimates the crossing by up to one integer step.
principles.regime: When nc varies with B (chunk-size boundary crossings)
principles.mechanism: num_iterations_per_prefill(B) is a step function that increases when the batch-size-to-chunk-size ratio crosses an integer boundary. The dev set has nc=1 throughout [1,256]; the benchmark has cases where nc jumps at high M (e.g., bench-025 nc=67 at M=256).
principles.applicability_bounds: Dev set: nc=1 confirmed. Benchmark: check nc before applying closed form.

principles.statement: M_TPF (largest B with ttft_prefill(B)<=targetTTFT) is an UPPER bound on the TTFT-CONSTRAINT-binding M (where the queue solver's full TTFT first exceeds targetTTFT), NOT an upper bound on the throughput argmax M_truth. The bracket can miss M_truth (ttft-only: M_truth=92 > M_TPF=70) while seed_upper at M_TPF still scores near-zero gap_throughput_rel, because the plateau onset is below M_TPF.
principles.regime: TTFT-binding scenarios where queue wait lifts the true feasible frontier above M_TPF
principles.mechanism: ttft_prefill(B) excludes the M/M/1 queue wait. The actual TTFT includes this wait, so the real TTFT constraint binds at M > M_TPF. Iter-4 h-robustness live confirms: seeding f* at m_max=256 instead of U=70 keeps threshold at true peak, allowing onset search to find M=72 (gap_M=20) rather than collapsing to M~56 (gap_M~36) from undershooting f*.
principles.applicability_bounds: ttft-only cell where M_TPF < M_ITL. In itl-or-crossover cells, M_TPF >= M_ITL and U=M_TPF is reliable as a seed.

principles.statement: The analytic primitives partition params-space into exactly THREE primitive-decidable cells, not four. The 'itl-only' and 'crossover' regimes are indistinguishable from primitives alone — they share an identical primitive signature (itl_binds=1, ttft_pf_binds=0, M_TPF>M_ITL).
principles.mechanism: Cells: (1) unbounded (neither binds), (2) ttft-only (M_TPF < M_ITL), (3) itl-or-crossover (M_ITL <= M_TPF). itl-only and crossover share cell 3.
principles.applicability_bounds: Primitive-level classification only. The oracle can further distinguish sub-cells at runtime.

principles.statement: The relative ORDER of M_TPF and M_ITL — specifically whether M_TPF < M_ITL — is the load-bearing classification signal for the ttft-only regime. The two binding booleans alone are insufficient: the ttft-only scenario binds on both (itl(256)>targetITL AND ttft_prefill(256)>targetTTFT), so a naive 2x2 on booleans misclassifies it as crossover.
principles.regime: ttft-only cell (TTFT constraint more restrictive than ITL)
principles.mechanism: For ttft-only scenario: M_ITL=95 > M_TPF=70. Both itl_binds and ttft_pf_binds are True (itl(256)=30.2>targetITL=60? Actually let me not hallucinate values). The key is M_TPF<M_ITL is the signal. Iter-4 confirms the branch fires correctly, triggering seed=m_max and yielding gap_M=20 (vs ~36 without it).
principles.applicability_bounds: Required in formula_guided.py to select cell-aware seed. Implemented as: ttft_only_cell = (M_TPF < M_ITL).

principles.statement: A downward monotone-predicate binary search anchored at m_max for f* (not the occupancy-bounded U) achieves worst-case calls=8 (seed + MAX_ITERS=6 + harness-confirmatory) on the 30-scenario benchmark while achieving worst gap_M=72 and worst gap_f=0.0138 — both strictly within the epsilon=0.02 constraint. The high-anchor (always m_max) is necessary and sufficient to fix the occupancy-gap bench-023 violation: reverting to the iter-4 U-seed restores gap_f=0.2238 on bench-023 while leaving all other scenarios unchanged. Pareto-dominates both naive_max and naive_ternary in aggregate gap_M.
principles.regime: 30-scenario benchmark including occupancy-gap, fully-infeasible, and interior-peak/nc-jump regimes; m_min=1, m_max=256; EPS=0.02; MAX_ITERS=6
principles.mechanism: High-anchor necessity: bench-023's U=max(M_ITL,M_TPF)=9 underestimates the plateau peak because the M/M/1 queue wait lifts realized throughput above the per-iteration binding B (RP-2). f*(U=9) << f*(57=peak), threshold set too low, descent terminates at M=9 (gap_f=0.22). m_max anchor reads f*(256)≈f*(57), threshold correct, descent finds onset~13 (gap_f=0.0037). Infeasibility detection: on 5 fully-infeasible scenarios (every /target 400s) fstar=0 at m_max -> return m_min=1=M_truth (gap_M=0). Without this, gap_M=255 on all 5.
principles.applicability_bounds: Applies when f is non-decreasing to a plateau (nc=1 or nc-jump with post-peak decline < EPS). Does not apply when the plateau is absent (non-concave f with deep valleys); in that case multi-probe f* over several candidate M values would be needed.

principles.statement: A parameter-blind ternary search (naive_ternary) achieves gap_f~0 on all dev scenarios (finds some plateau point) but fails on the concurrency objective: it converges to an interior/high plateau point rather than the onset. On the dev set: worst gap_M=127 (ttft-only: M=219 vs M*=92, alpha-high: M=40 vs M*=17) at 30 calls per scenario. The formula's downward-onset targeting — not search per se — is the component that delivers low gap_M.
principles.regime: nc=1 dev set; monotone-to-plateau f curves
principles.mechanism: Ternary search maximizes f(M). On a long flat plateau, probing m1=lo+(hi-lo)//3 and m2=hi-(hi-lo)//3 yields near-equal f values near the plateau, causing the comparisons to be driven by floating-point noise. The search converges to a point near the middle or high end of the plateau, never to the onset (the SMALLEST plateau point). Without a lower-bound bias from the closed-form formulas, there is no mechanism to push the result toward the onset.
principles.applicability_bounds: Any ternary/golden-section search on a monotone-to-plateau function. The gap_M win of formula_guided is robust to this failure mode because the downward predicate search explicitly targets the smallest M satisfying the threshold.

principles.statement: The cache M_truth is the argmax (smallest m reaching the strict float-maximum throughput), not the 0.98-onset. On the dev set's float-flat plateau argmax == onset, but on the benchmark's wiggly plateau they diverge significantly (bench-019 onset~46 vs argmax=118; bench-023 onset~13 vs argmax=57). A concurrency-correct onset finder carries a structural gap_M floor on wide-plateau scenarios that no strategy can close without over-provisioning to the argmax. This floor must be reported per-regime and is not strategy error.
principles.regime: 30-scenario benchmark; any scenario where the plateau wiggles at float precision such that the strict argmax sits well above the 0.98-onset
principles.mechanism: baseline_truth.py computes M_truth as the smallest m where throughput is strictly greater than the previous best (if t > best_t). On a float-flat dev plateau the first non-zero throughput point is both argmax and onset. On the benchmark's wiggly plateau the float-exact maximum sits at an interior point (e.g. bench-019: plateau runs M=16..256 but float maximum is at M=118). Scoring computes gap_M=abs(M_chosen-M_truth); a correct onset finder (M=46) scores gap_M=72 against the argmax (M=118) even though 46 is a perfectly valid concurrency choice.
principles.applicability_bounds: Relevant whenever gap_M is used as the evaluation metric and the plateau has float-level wiggles. Applies to the current harness/scoring.py; would not apply if gap_M were scored against a fixed 0.98-onset.

principles.statement: Parameter-blind ternary search (maximizing throughput over [m_min, m_max]) returns gap_M=0 on fully-infeasible scenarios as a behavioral coincidence: when all /target calls return 400 (throughput=0), f1==f2 at every ternary step so hi always narrows, converging to lo=m_min=M_truth=1. This is not an explicit infeasibility check — it consumes 0 counted calls (400s not counted by oracle.py) and would not hold if the ternary comparison logic were reversed or if m_min != M_truth.
principles.regime: Fully-infeasible scenarios (every /target returns HTTP 400); m_min=1; oracle.py does not count 400 responses toward stats.calls
principles.mechanism: naive_ternary.py: while hi-lo>2, m1=lo+(hi-lo)//3, m2=hi-(hi-lo)//3, f1=f2=0 so else branch: hi=m2. hi narrows monotonically toward lo. Final for-loop over [lo,hi+1] finds all throughputs 0, so best stays at lo=m_min=1. With M_truth=m_min=1, gap_M=0. This is an artifact of the else-branch preference narrowing hi rather than lo when f1==f2.
principles.applicability_bounds: Holds only when (1) ALL target_eval calls return 0, (2) m_min == M_truth, (3) the ternary breaks ties by narrowing hi. Would not hold on partially-infeasible scenarios or if the tie-breaking were different.
